current_fact.fact_text: On May 14, 1832, the Battle of Stillman’s Run marked the chaotic opening clash of the Black Hawk War in Illinois, when poorly disciplined militia forces panicked and fled from a much smaller group of Native American warriors. The skirmish became a cautionary tale about miscommunication, fear, and frontier conflict.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

current_fact.match_reason: A violent frontier clash rooted in power and dispossession fits an episode centered on Rage Against the Machine, whose music directly confronts militarism and colonial injustice.

queue.fact_text: On May 14, 1747, during the War of the Austrian Succession, a British fleet under Admiral George Anson defeated a French convoy at the First Battle of Cape Finisterre. Beyond the naval glory, the capture of French ships carrying treasure and supplies underscored how sea control could tilt an entire continental war.

episode.title: Morning Drops #22 - Sicilians On The Dolomites
episode.description: Hosted by Yasmin, a musical journey inspired by the mountains. Joined by two special guests, exploring the tracks they've chosen, hearing the stories behind their picks, and discovering the personal memories each song brings to life — a blend of music, voices, and landscapes you can hear.
songs: Florian Pellissier Quintet - Coup de foudre à Thessalonique
Lucio Dalla & Francesco De Gregori - Ma come fanno i marinai

queue.match_reason: A naval battle off a stormy cape links nicely to an episode featuring 'Ma come fanno i marinai' (How do the sailors do it), blending seafaring imagery and shifting landscapes.

queue.fact_text: On May 14, 1915, the May 14 Revolt erupted in Lisbon as military and civilian factions rose against Portugal’s government amid deep political instability. The uprising briefly toppled a president, illustrating how fragile young republics can be when ideals collide with power struggles.

episode.title: Morning Drops #52- Think Again
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the quiet art of changing your mind, guided by Adam Grant's book Think Again. From the beliefs we carry without questioning to the identities we build around what we think we know, this episode follows the slow, sometimes uncomfortable process of unlearning.

Along the way, we meet a Serbian trumpet player who had to let go of one dream to find another, a Tunisian oud master who challenged centuries of tradition from the inside, and a Japanese band who found freedom in playing for no one. The music moves from Balkan brass to Mediterranean stillness to psychedelic drift, tracing the shape of a mind opening up.

Listen slowly, and ask yourself: is there something you believe today that deserves a second look?

Tracklist:
Boban Marković Orkestar – SAT
Anouar Brahem – The Astounding Eyes of Rita
Kikagaku Moyo – Dripping Sun
Son Lux – Lost It to Trying

queue.match_reason: A coup born from rigid ideology and political miscalculation contrasts with an episode about revising beliefs and unlearning, inviting listeners to imagine a different path than revolt.

queue.fact_text: On May 14, 1940, the German Luftwaffe bombed Rotterdam despite ongoing ceasefire negotiations, killing around 900 people and obliterating the historic city center. The devastation became a grim symbol of "terror bombing" and forced the Netherlands’ rapid capitulation in World War II.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

queue.match_reason: A city reduced to rubble by bombs echoes the metaphorical storms in 'It Can't Rain Forever', where the focus is on surviving devastation and holding on to hope.

queue.fact_text: On May 14, 1943, the Australian hospital ship AHS Centaur was torpedoed off the coast of Queensland by a Japanese submarine, killing 268 of the 332 people on board. The attack on a clearly marked medical vessel shocked the public and later fueled debates over wartime rules and accountability.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

queue.match_reason: A wartime sinking off a distant coast and an episode about quiet encounters with whales both center on powerful, dangerous waters and the stories hidden beneath the waves.

queue.fact_text: On May 14, 1955, eight Eastern Bloc countries led by the Soviet Union signed the Warsaw Pact, a mutual defense treaty that formalized a military alliance opposite NATO. For decades, the pact structured the military and political landscape of the Cold War, binding member states into a tightly controlled bloc.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

queue.match_reason: A rigid military alliance built on surveillance and control contrasts with an episode questioning our own invisible alliances to technology and constant connection.

queue.fact_text: On May 14, 1953, about 7,100 brewery workers in Milwaukee walked off their jobs, launching the 1953 Milwaukee brewery strike. The labor action, which lasted weeks, reshaped wages, union power, and even the city’s reputation as "Beer Town" as taps ran dry and loyalty to local brands was tested.

episode.title: Morning Drops #01 - Timeless Legacy
episode.description: Gaia guides us through timeless music to start the morning with the right foot! The first episode of the Morning Drops series — classic sounds that never fade.
songs: Roberta Flack - Killing me softly with his song

queue.match_reason: A strike challenging how a city works pairs with an episode about 'timeless' classics and legacy, hinting that worker struggles and their cultural impact can also become part of a city’s enduring soundtrack.

queue.fact_text: On May 14, 1977, a Dan-Air Boeing 707 operating for IAS Cargo Airlines crashed on approach to Lusaka International Airport in Zambia, killing six people. The accident highlighted the risks of long-haul cargo flights and the complexities of nighttime approaches over challenging terrain.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

queue.match_reason: A tragic aviation accident born from speed and pressure is juxtaposed with an episode advocating a 'slow planet', inviting listeners to imagine gentler rhythms in our global movements.

queue.fact_text: On May 14, 1980, during the Salvadoran Civil War, the Sumpul River massacre unfolded as Salvadoran and Honduran forces attacked civilians trying to flee across the river, killing hundreds. Survivors’ testimonies turned a remote river crossing into a symbol of wartime brutality and contested memory.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

queue.match_reason: A river massacre entwined with displacement and survival is paired with an episode about Tuareg music, itself born from exile, memory, and resistance in harsh landscapes.

queue.fact_text: On May 14, 2004, Rico Linhas Aéreas Flight 4815 crashed into Brazil’s Amazon rainforest during approach to Manaus, killing all 33 people on board. The accident emphasized how even short regional flights can be vulnerable to weather, terrain, and the vast, unforgiving canopy of the world’s largest rainforest.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

queue.match_reason: An air disaster over dense rainforest contrasts with a contemplative ocean episode, yet both explore how powerful ecosystems—canopy or Arctic sea—can be beautiful and utterly unforgiving.

queue.fact_text: On May 14, 2008, Manchester’s Piccadilly Gardens became the center of pitched street battles when Zenit St Petersburg and Rangers supporters clashed with each other and police around the UEFA Cup final. The riots injured dozens of officers and exposed how football fandom can suddenly spill into urban chaos.

episode.title: Morning Drops #51 - The Dancefloor
episode.description:
For International Women’s Day, this Morning Drop turns its focus to women on the dancefloor, not as a trend, but as a cultural force.

Across five tracks, Gaia follows a line from disco closers to hip hop club classics, tracing how the dancefloor became a place where women could take space, build confidence, and create connection, sometimes with friends, sometimes alone, always with music at the centre.

This episode isn’t about pointing fingers. It’s a gentle reminder that spaces like the dancefloor still matter: places where people can express themselves, meet each other with respect, and feel part of something bigger than a night out.

Tracklist:
Donna Summer - Last Dance (1978)
The Sequence  - Funk you up
Salt-N-Pepa - Push It
Queen Latifah - Ladies First
Alicia Bridges - I Love the Nightlife

queue.match_reason: Violent clashes around a sporting spectacle contrast with an episode reimagining shared public spaces—like the dancefloor—as places of respect, joy, and safer collective energy.

queue.fact_text: On May 14, 1951, the Talyllyn Railway’s volunteer-run trains quietly inaugurated a new model of heritage preservation; but ten years later, on the same date in 1961, nothing comparable happened on those rails—reminding us that some days are defined by what is preserved, not what is built. The Talyllyn story shows that even small lines can leave long shadows in transport history.

episode.title: Sedna FM #05 - On The Go - Zero Zero
episode.description: An energetic mix for when you're on the move. Zero Zero captures that feeling of starting fresh, moving forward, and embracing the journey. Perfect for travel, walks, or just getting things done.
songs: Grace Jones - Pull up to the bumper
Easy star all star - Soul love
James Brown - Talking loud and saying nothing (remix)

queue.match_reason: A preserved railway that keeps people literally 'on the go' pairs with an episode designed for movement, travel, and the joy of staying in motion.

queue.fact_text: On May 14, 1951, volunteers saved one tiny Welsh railway; four years later on May 14, 1955, governments tried to lock entire countries into a massive alliance via the Warsaw Pact. Together, these episodes of coordination—from grassroots to geopolitical—show how collective agreements can shape both local tracks and global trajectories.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

queue.match_reason: The explosive geopolitical weight of the Warsaw Pact matches the raw, wide-ranging energy of Sedna’s very first 'bomb' of an episode—a foundational alliance of sounds.

queue.fact_text: On May 14, 2008, as football fans fought in Manchester, another kind of crowd was forming online—one that would soon turn reggaeton, Afrobeat, and trap into global forces. History reminds us that while some gatherings implode, others remix cultures into new shared rhythms.

episode.title: Morning Drops #13 - Demystifying Reggaeton
episode.description: Yasmin takes us on a journey through the real story of reggaeton — from its roots in Panama and Puerto Rico to its global evolution. Exploring the genre's history, influences, and cultural impact.
songs: Renato - Chica de los Ojos Café
B. F. Yaviah - Si Tu Quieres Fumar

queue.match_reason: Rowdy football crowds and explosive party music intersect in questions of how bodies move together; this episode reframes that energy through the lens of reggaeton’s history instead of violence.

queue.fact_text: On May 14, 2021, the Zhurong rover touched Martian soil; centuries earlier, on May 14, 1796, Jenner probed the microscopic world of viruses. From cowpox blisters to Martian sand grains, this date has quietly hosted leaps in how humans explore the unseen.

episode.title: Morning Drops #23 - Morning Dubs
episode.description: Recorded while still processing Houghton Festival. One of the mornings there, waking up in the woods to a reggae DJ set — no phone signal, just trees, music, and loved ones. A slow selection of reggae, dub, and morning rhythms. Dubs instead of drops. Start your day slow — with space to breathe.
songs: The Gaylads - Joy in the Morning
King Tubby - King Tubby Meets the Rockers Uptown

queue.match_reason: Microscopic vaccines and distant Martian soils both rely on patient, low-frequency exploration—mirrored by dub’s spacious, echoing soundscapes that leave room for the unseen to resonate.

published.fact_text: On May 14, 1973, NASA launched Skylab, the United States’ first space station, into orbit using a modified Saturn V rocket. Although it suffered damage during launch, astronauts later performed in-space repairs, turning it into a pioneering laboratory for long-duration human spaceflight and solar science.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A space-station launch pairs naturally with an episode built around reimagining 'space' as possibility, both literally and metaphorically.

published.fact_text: On May 14, 2010, Space Shuttle Atlantis lifted off on mission STS-132, carrying the Russian-built Rassvet module—the only Russian segment of the ISS delivered by a US shuttle. The flight was intended as Atlantis’s final mission, giving it a bittersweet 'last dance' aura before one more surprise mission was later approved.

episode.title: Morning Drops #48 - Cosmic Monday
episode.description:
Cosmic Monday is a Morning Drops episode built around four Italian dance records, tracing how disco rhythms slowly became electronic language.
Gaia moves through four tracks like chapters: groove first, then machines, then that bright, futuristic feeling you only get when the bassline is doing the talking. It's not nostalgia, exactly. It's more like returning to a sound that still works — especially on mornings when you need a little extra energy, but you don't want anything aggressive.
Take it as a slow transmission. Let it carry you into the day.
songs: Giovanotti Mondani Meccanici - Don't Ask Me Why

published.match_reason: A shuttle mission carrying a space station module fits a 'cosmic' themed set that traces futuristic electronic rhythms and orbital moods.

published.fact_text: On May 14, 2021, China’s Zhurong rover successfully landed on Mars, making China only the second nation to softly land and operate a rover on the Red Planet. Named after a fire god, Zhurong began exploring the Utopia Planitia region, studying Martian geology, climate, and subsurface structures.

episode.title: Evening Flows #01 - Yasmin - Crescendo in the Medina
episode.description: The first Evening Flow episode comes from Yasmin. It starts deep in the medina, slow, mysterious, textured, and grows into a liberating dance across sub-Saharan landscapes. A journey through ambient soundscapes, Arabic melodies, Afro-Latin rhythms, and funky highlife grooves.
songs: David Visan & Carlos Campos - Crystal Heart
Evan Mars - Ya Zareef Al Tool
Dele Sosimi & Medlar - You No Fit Touch Am (Medlar Remix)

published.match_reason: A mysterious, solar-wind rover landing on Mars resonates with an ambient, space-tinged evening mix that literally opens on 'Solar Wind' and grows into exploratory grooves.

published.fact_text: On May 14, 1796, English physician Edward Jenner administered the first successful smallpox inoculation by deliberately infecting eight-year-old James Phipps with cowpox. The boy’s subsequent immunity to smallpox led to the birth of vaccination and, eventually, to the global eradication of one of humanity’s deadliest diseases.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

published.match_reason: Jenner’s risky experiment was a radical act of scientific learning, aligning with an episode about the emotional landscape of trial, error, and discovery.

published.fact_text: On May 14, 1804, William Clark departed Camp Dubois with 42 men to meet Meriwether Lewis at St. Charles, Missouri, officially setting the Lewis and Clark Expedition in motion up the Missouri River. Their journey would map vast swaths of North America and forever alter the continent’s geographic imagination.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

published.match_reason: An 1800s river expedition mirrors a modern boat journey episode, both centered on long voyages, shared crews, and the rhythms of travelling over water.

published.fact_text: On May 14, 1951, the Talyllyn Railway in Wales ran its first trains entirely under volunteer management, becoming the world’s first preserved railway operated by enthusiasts. Their efforts not only saved a narrow-gauge line from closure but also sparked a global heritage railway movement.

episode.title: Morning Drops #35 - Thailand
episode.description: Recorded in Koh Samui, Thailand, where Yasmin and her friend Patricia spent more than two intense weeks training Muay Thai and Western boxing. Between sweat, rhythm, and sea breeze, they share reflections on discipline, friendship, and the beauty of movement - with a soundtrack that connects Thai sounds to the flow of daily practice.
songs: Burna Boy - Cheat on Me (feat. Dave)

published.match_reason: A volunteer-run railway and a disciplined training retreat both celebrate everyday people dedicating time and energy to keep beloved practices moving.

published.fact_text: On May 14, 1878, Salem, Massachusetts hosted the last witchcraft-related trial in the United States, when Lucretia Brown accused Daniel Spofford of harming her through "mental malpractice" under Christian Science beliefs. The case, echoing the city’s infamous past, showed how fears of unseen forces can resurface in new spiritual languages.

episode.title: Morning Drops #38 - History of Flamenco
episode.description: A special journey into the history of Flamenco, guided by Yasmin and her guest Almudena from Madrid, Spain. Exploring the roots and evolution of this emotionally raw and culturally rich tradition, from its early vocal origins and Andalusian influences, through its transformation on stage and screen, to its modern reinventions by iconic Spanish artists.
songs: La Niña de los Peines - Al Gurugú
Paco de Lucía - Entre dos aguas
Camarón de la Isla - La leyenda del tiempo

published.match_reason: A witchcraft trial rooted in fear and folklore connects to an episode about Flamenco, a tradition steeped in superstition, duende, and intense emotional mysticism.

published.fact_text: On May 14, 1879, the ship Leonidas arrived in Fiji carrying the first group of 463 Indian indentured laborers, inaugurating a system that would profoundly reshape Fiji’s demographics and culture. Their descendants now form a significant part of the country’s population, blending Indian and Pacific traditions into a unique Indo-Fijian identity.

episode.title: Morning Drops #11 - Mediterranean Boat
episode.description: Gaia invites you to embark on a musical voyage across the Mediterranean. Exploring music from different countries shared by people who call them home. A warm, sun-soaked journey through coastal sounds.
songs: Fabrizio De André - Crêuza de mä

published.match_reason: A sea voyage that permanently reshaped a country’s culture aligns with an episode that sails across coastal sounds and the feeling of carrying home across waters.

published.fact_text: On May 14, 1811, Paraguayan leaders Pedro Juan Caballero, Fulgencio Yegros, and José Gaspar Rodríguez de Francia began coordinated actions to depose the Spanish governor in Asunción. Their movement led to Paraguay’s independence and to an experiment in isolationist nation-building under Francia’s austere rule.

episode.title: Morning Drops #44 - Residente
episode.description:
In this edition of Morning Drops, Yasmin navigates the complex geography of René Pérez Joglar, known to the world as Residente. His story is not just a musical evolution; it is a timeline marked by resistance, lyrical obsession, and a relentless search for identity.

From the gated streets of Trujillo Alto to the global stage, we explore how a restless mind transformed into the voice of a generation. The selection moves through the textures of his life: hearing the raw energy of his early rebellion, the sharp precision of his political critique, and finally, the fragile silence of his most personal confessions.

It is a study in duality. We witness the rapper who dismantles giants and the son who misses his mother. It is a soundscape that proves that sometimes, the loudest revolution happens inside.

Mood: Raw, Revolutionary, Introspective, Honest
Music genre: Latin Alternative, Hip Hop, Urban, World

published.match_reason: An early 19th-century Latin American independence movement pairs with an episode about Residente, an artist obsessed with decolonial politics, identity, and Latin American resistance.

published.fact_text: On May 14, 1832, the Battle of Stillman’s Run marked the chaotic opening clash of the Black Hawk War in Illinois, when poorly disciplined militia forces panicked and fled from a much smaller group of Native American warriors. The skirmish became a cautionary tale about miscommunication, fear, and frontier conflict.
